repo.name: signal-board
repo.description: A public Next.js analytics dashboard for tracking product signals and lightweight incident notes.

architectureSummary: Signal Board appears to be a frontend-heavy Next.js application with App Router pages, reusable dashboard components, and a small API layer. The structure is understandable, but data access, UI state, and product workflows should be made more explicit before the repository grows.

importantFiles.reason: Defines product intent, local setup, and the demo workflow.
Shows framework choice, scripts, and core runtime dependencies.
Primary landing route and first user-facing workflow.
Main server route where external data enters the app.
Composes the core dashboard experience and state boundaries.
Documents required runtime configuration without exposing secrets.
Controls design tokens and responsive behavior.
Defines TypeScript strictness and import paths.

agents.summary: The app has a clean Next.js shape, but the boundaries between routes, dashboard composition, and data normalization need firmer ownership.
topFindings: The App Router layout is easy to enter from app/page.tsx.
Dashboard components are grouped well enough for a first pass.
API and UI data contracts should be centralized before adding more panels.

risks.title: Loose data boundary
risks.explanation: If API responses drift from dashboard expectations, visual components will accumulate defensive checks.

risks.title: Feature growth pressure
risks.explanation: New dashboard sections may land as route-level logic unless shared composition rules are established.

recommendations: Define shared result types before expanding the dashboard.
Keep API transformation in lib/ rather than inside page components.
Document the route and component ownership model in README.md.

agents.summary: The likely failure points are API response shape mismatches, missing environment variables, and loading states that do not recover from partial failure.
topFindings: The dashboard path depends on complete analysis data being present.
Environment-driven features need graceful fallback states.
Local demo data is a strong recovery mechanism if kept schema-compatible.

risks.title: Loading dead end
risks.explanation: A failed fetch could leave users waiting unless the scan route always routes to a valid result.

risks.title: Silent malformed data
risks.explanation: AI-generated JSON or API changes can create blank sections without a clear user-facing error.

recommendations: Add a hard timeout around live analysis.
Validate result shape before writing to localStorage.
Keep a manual demo mode CTA visible on landing and empty dashboard states.

agents.summary: The repository has a small attack surface, but any server route that calls third-party APIs must protect keys and avoid echoing raw provider failures.
topFindings: No private repository support reduces credential complexity.
.env.example is the correct place to document keys without exposing them.
Server-only provider calls should stay inside API routes and lib modules.

risks.title: Provider error leakage
risks.explanation: Raw AI or GitHub errors can reveal configuration details if returned directly to the browser.

risks.title: Unbounded repo input
risks.explanation: Large repositories can create expensive or slow requests unless the parser samples only important files.

recommendations: Never expose GEMINI_API_KEY, GROQ_API_KEY, or GITHUB_TOKEN in client code.
Return clean fallback responses instead of raw stack traces.
Limit fetched file previews by path, size, and count.

agents.summary: The product story is strong when it stays focused on fast repo understanding and a prioritized fix plan, not account features.
topFindings: The council metaphor makes the analysis feel more valuable than a README summary.
Risk score, debate, and fix plan create a clear demo arc.
The strongest next feature is better repo-specific action prioritization, not saved history.

risks.title: Scope dilution
risks.explanation: Login, profiles, and SaaS settings would distract from the core hackathon proof.

risks.title: Generic recommendations
risks.explanation: The dashboard loses credibility if findings do not mention concrete files or repo structure.

recommendations: Keep the first screen centered on pasting a public GitHub URL.
Make fallback mode feel intentional and demo-safe.
Prioritize concise, file-aware output over long AI prose.

comments.position: Stabilize structure before adding more dashboard modules.
comments.reasoning: The existing layout is understandable, but feature additions should not blur route, data, and presentation boundaries.
A hackathon demo cannot afford an endless loading state or malformed result screen.

comments.position: Keep all external API calls server-side and aggressively sampled.
comments.reasoning: The app only needs public repository context, so there is no reason to increase credential exposure.

comments.position: Ship the command-center review loop and reject SaaS bloat.
comments.reasoning: The user value is understanding a repo in minutes, not managing an account.

debate.finalDecision: Prioritize a reliable paste-to-dashboard flow: validate public GitHub URLs, sample important files, run four concise agents, and always fall back to a complete demo report if live analysis fails.

understandFirst: Read README.md to confirm product promise and local setup.
Inspect package.json scripts and dependencies before running the app.
Open app/page.tsx and dashboard composition files to trace the primary workflow.
fixFirst: Guarantee fallback mode after API, GitHub, or AI provider failure.
Add validation around repository URL input and provider JSON output.
buildNext: Improve repo-specific file explanations using important file previews.
Add clearer risk scoring explanations in the dashboard.
Tune agent prompts for shorter, more decisive findings.
ignoreForNow: Saved report history or database persistence.
Automatic code modification or pull request creation.
